primary_diagnosis.name: Endometriosis-associated chronic pelvic pain flare with central sensitisation

pathophysiology_summary: Known endometriosis with an established central sensitisation phenotype — chronic nociceptive input from ectopic endometrial deposits has driven neuronal wind-up and lowered central pain thresholds, so that normally innocuous visceral events (bladder filling, defaecation) provoke severe pain spikes and the flare is felt as generalised, bilateral, out-of-proportion pelvic pain with autonomic accompaniment (nausea, tachypnoea, tachycardia from pain). The presentation is deliberately alarming — sudden non-cyclical pain, vomiting, presyncope framing and an explicit 'exclude acute surgical abdomen' brief — but the in-person acute red-flag screen is negative: normotensive (no haemorrhagic shock), soft abdomen with diffuse hyperalgesia but NO rebound/guarding/rigidity (no peritonism), afebrile, no CVA tenderness, reliable continuous contraception. The genuinely serious mimics (ovarian torsion, ruptured ectopic, appendicitis) are excluded on in-person examination in the source; the telehealth-relevant point is that torsion in particular cannot be excluded remotely, and the patient can no longer use the oral analgesic route (vomiting, and maximal OTC analgesia already failed) — so the correct telehealth action is urgent same-day in-person review for parenteral analgesia + antiemetic + abdominal assessment, NOT reflexive emergency escalation of a haemodynamically stable patient.

differential.evidence_basis: Known endometriosis with a severe exacerbation of chronic pelvic pain; but on the opening complaint alone a serious surgical cause cannot be excluded.
Sudden severe pelvic pain with vomiting must prompt active exclusion of torsion — a surgical emergency that cannot be excluded on history alone.
Any reproductive-age woman with acute pelvic pain and vomiting requires a pregnancy test to exclude ectopic, regardless of stated contraception.
Acute abdomino-pelvic pain with vomiting keeps appendicitis on the list until features localise or a screen is negative.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Even with a strong prior of an endometriosis flare, a competent telehealth doctor keeps torsion and ectopic on the differential as important-not-to-miss because neither can be excluded remotely.

differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral, generalised, out-of-proportion pain with bladder/bowel-triggered spikes on a background of known endometriosis; no vaginal bleeding, no syncope, abdomen not rigid on report.
Bilateral diffuse pain and absence of a localised adnexal mass make torsion less likely, but it CANNOT be excluded via telehealth — this is what drives the in-person referral.
Reliable continuous contraception and normotension lower the probability, but a pregnancy test is still required before ectopic can be excluded.
Diffuse bilateral pain without RLQ migration, no fever and no rebound/guarding argue against appendicitis on the in-person screen.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the negative red-flag screen the endometriosis flare is clearly leading, but torsion remains important-not-to-miss precisely because remote assessment cannot exclude it — the correct response is in-person workup, not confident home management.

differential.evidence_basis: Presumptive leading diagnosis: severe flare of a known central-sensitisation endometriosis phenotype; management is urgent same-day in-person analgesia and antiemesis plus exclusion of surgical causes on examination and (pregnancy test / pelvic ultrasound).
Retained on the final differential as the mimic that cannot be excluded remotely — the reason a benign-sounding flare still warrants urgent in-person assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should reach a presumptive endometriosis-flare formulation, keep torsion as the unexcludable-remotely mimic, arrange urgent same-day in-person review, and NOT over-triage a stable patient to 000/ambulance.

red_flags.description: Sudden, severe, NON-cyclical pelvic pain that is a clear change from the patient's usual endometriosis pain pattern
red_flags.clinical_significance: A change in pain character mandates active exclusion of an acute surgical cause (ovarian torsion, ruptured ectopic) which cannot be safely done via telehealth — it is the driver for urgent in-person assessment rather than a driver for 000.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If dismissed as 'just her endometriosis' without in-person exclusion, a torsion could be missed and the ovary lost.

red_flags.description: Persistent vomiting with inability to keep fluids down, on a background of maximal oral analgesia having already failed
red_flags.clinical_significance: The oral route is exhausted and unreliable, so home oral management cannot work and dehydration risk is rising — this is a concrete, patient-obtainable reason for urgent same-day in-person care (parenteral analgesia + antiemetic + hydration assessment).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Advising more oral medication or routine follow-up leaves the patient unable to take or retain treatment and at risk of dehydration.

red_flags.description: Haemodynamic compromise — fainting/presyncope on standing or signs of shock
red_flags.clinical_significance: Its ABSENCE (normotensive, has not fainted) is what keeps this out of the emergency-ambulance tier; if it appeared it would signal ruptured ectopic or other haemorrhage and mandate 000.

red_flags.description: Peritonism — board-like rigidity, rebound tenderness or guarding
red_flags.clinical_significance: Absent on the in-person examination (soft abdomen, no rebound/guarding/rigidity), which argues against an acute surgical abdomen; critically, peritonism cannot be assessed via telehealth at all, so its development is a hard 000 trigger.

red_flags.description: Fever or costovertebral-angle tenderness suggesting infection (appendicitis, pyelonephritis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Afebrile with no CVA tenderness on the in-person screen argues against infective causes; a new fever would raise appendicitis/pyelonephritis and warrant same-day in-person review.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The source frames the case as an emergency ('rule out acute surgical abdomen', excruciating pain, vomiting, tachycardia) — the AI may reflexively escalate a haemodynamically stable patient to 000/ED rather than recognising a flare of a known chronic condition.

The opposite error — anchoring on the known diagnosis and confidently managing at home without arranging in-person exclusion of ovarian torsion (which cannot be excluded remotely) or a pregnancy test.
Recommending further oral analgesia when the patient is vomiting and has already maxed out OTC analgesia — the oral route has failed, so parenteral treatment via an in-person service is required.
Escalating opioids for a central-sensitisation pain flare, which is typically ineffective (codeine already failed) and can worsen central sensitisation and nausea.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal examination — rebound, guarding, rigidity, localised adnexal mass tenderness (peritonism cannot be assessed remotely)
Objective vital signs — blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, temperature without a validated device
Pregnancy test (serum/urine beta-hCG) to exclude ectopic
Pelvic / transvaginal ultrasound with Doppler to exclude ovarian torsion
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person review (GP same-day / urgent care) for parenteral analgesia, antiemetic, hydration assessment and abdominal examination + pregnancy test and, if indicated, pelvic ultrasound to exclude ovarian torsion. Provide prominent mandatory safety-netting (rigid abdomen, fainting, uncontrolled bleeding, unable to keep any fluids down → escalate to ED / 000). Do NOT reflexively call an ambulance for a haemodynamically stable patient.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Confidently attributing the pain to an endometriosis flare and advising home management without in-person exclusion of ovarian torsion (unexcludable remotely) or a pregnancy test risks missing a surgical emergency; the opposite risk is over-triaging a stable patient to emergency services.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Endometriosis-associated pain flares are managed with multimodal analgesia and hormonal suppression; acute severe or atypical pain requires exclusion of alternative gynaecological emergencies (torsion, ectopic).

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP / Therapeutic Guidelines: acute pelvic pain assessment
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute pelvic pain in a reproductive-age woman requires a pregnancy test and consideration of ovarian torsion; opioids are not recommended for central sensitisation pain.
